source_metadata.title_raw: Andrew Morris, Esq. - A-18-117 - August 15, 2018 - Mammoth Lakes

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 15, 2018

Andrew Morris, Esq.
Town Attorney
Town of Mammoth Lakes
P. O. Box 1609
Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-117

Dear Mr. Morris:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act")¹ on behalf of Town of Mammoth Lakes Councilmember Colin Fernie.

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. In addition, the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

Considering his ski rental business and nearby property interest, does Town Councilmember Colin Fernie have a conflict of interest under the Act with respect to participating in governmental decisions related to a new multiuse facility ("MUF") to be constructed in the Town?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Fernie may take part in the decisions under the public generally exception because the decisions will affect at least 25 percent of the businesses in the jurisdiction and will not uniquely affect the Councilmember's rental business, property, or customers.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-117
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You serve as Town Attorney for the Town of Mammoth Lakes and seek advice regarding a
potential conflict of interest involving Town Councilmember Colin Fernie.

The Town owns a public park known as Mammoth Creek Park, which is approximately 4.9
acres in size. The western portion of the park is currently undeveloped. The town is in the process
of evaluating whether to construct an MUF on the western portion, which would consist of an ice
rink and related equipment and possibly a community center either initially or in a later phase of the
project. The design of the MUF has not been finalized, but it is anticipated to include parking
improvements, lighting, and a partial relocation of the park's existing playground.

If constructed, it is anticipated that the MUF would host events year-round and could help
increase visitation to Mammoth Lakes. Additionally, you have also if increasing tourism through
the addition of the MUF is one of the purposes of the project. You estimate that Mammoth's
economy is 90-95% dependent on tourism. The Town operates an ice rink already, but it is an
indoor rink that can be used only in the winter and is not full-sized. Constructing the MUF could
draw youth hockey teams from southern California that want to play or train at high altitude, and
that there would be hockey tournaments held in the new facility during much of the year.

Councilmember Fernie and his business partner own a limited liability company called
Kramerica Industries, LLC. In April 2018, the LLC acquired a commercial condominium space in
Mammoth Lakes ("Property"). The Property is located approximately 900 feet from the nearest
corner of the park, along one of the Town's main arterial roadways. There are numerous other
properties housing commercial and/or office uses near the Property.

The Property is currently leased to a ski rental business, Black Tie Ski Rentals of Mammoth
Lakes, CA, Inc., which is also owned by Councilmember Fernie and his business partner.
Councilmember Fernie has indicated that approximately 90 percent of his ski rental business
consists of delivering skis and equipment to customers at their lodgings, and this is the service
advertised on the business' website. The ski rental business gets a very small amount of business
from walk-in customers. The delivery feature is a core part of Councilmember Fernie's business
model and serves to distinguish his business from the numerous other ski rental shops in Mammoth
Lakes. The delivery service is extremely unlikely to change if the MUF were constructed. Increased
vehicular or pedestrian traffic along the road generated by the MUF project is not expected to
significantly affect Councilmember Fernie's business.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes,
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision that has a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests.

Section 87103 defines a "financial interest" as follows:

"A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,

File No. A-18-117
Page No. 3

distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the following:

"(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment
worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.

"(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.

"(c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made
in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to,
received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

"(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management."

Based upon the facts provided, it appears that Councilmember Fernie has an interest in the
Property and interests in the ski rental business as a business entity and source of income.²
Councilmember Fernie may also have an interest in customers of the ski rental business as sources
of income.

Foreseeability:

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)
Under the facts you have provided the Property and the ski rental business are not explicitly
involved in the decisions. Thus, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect
can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable."³

Materiality:

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides the materiality standard for an interest in a business entity,
including a source of income, not explicitly involved in the decision. (See Regulation 18702.3.)

² A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. A governmental decision will have an
effect on this interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or
his or her immediate family member increasing or decreasing. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. (Regulation 18702.5.)
If a governmental decision affects a business entity or real property in which an official has an interest, materiality is
determined by looking at the materiality standards for those interests instead. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Therefore, we do
not separately analyze Councilmember Fernie's interest in his personal finances.

³ Regulation 18701(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the
"reasonably foreseeable" determination.

File No. A-18-117
Page No. 4

Under Regulation 18702.1(b), a financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock or the value of a privately-held business entity, such as decisions that:

"(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies;

"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

"(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the project;"

You indicated that, if developed, the MUF would consist of an ice rink and related equipment and possibly a community center, which would include parking improvements, lighting, and a partial relocation of the park's existing playground. The Town Council will be tasked with deciding the MUF's size and scope, and if developed, the MUF would host year-round events. It is anticipated that the MUF would increase visitor visitation to the Town. Councilmember Fernie's business is engaged in the activity of delivering rental ski equipment to customers at their lodgings. Because the MUF is expected to increase the number of visitors to the Town, this would likely increase the number of people using lodging and increase the need for services his business provides. Due to the proximity of his business to the MUF site, the increased foot traffic could also increase the number of walk-in visitors. Therefore, it is foreseeable that the new MUF would result in more patrons seeking the services offered by the Councilmember's ski rental business. Thus, Councilmember Fernie has a disqualifying conflict of interest in taking part in MUF-related decisions.⁴

Public Generally Exception:

The Act prohibits an official from participating in a decision only if the decision's effect on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation 187034 permits public officials to take part in governmental decisions that affect their interests if the effect on the interest is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. Commonly, an effect on an official's interest is "indistinguishable" from its effect on the public generally if (1) a significant segment of the public is affected and (2) the effect on the official's interest is not unique when compared to the effect on the significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703(a).)

Regulation 18703(b)(1) and (2) provide that a significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of all business entities or commercial properties within the official's jurisdiction. A unique effect is defined as a disproportionate effect on:

⁴ In light of the conclusion that Councilmember Fernie has a disqualifying interest in his business entity, we do not further analyze his interest in his real property or customers of his business.

File No. A-18-117
Page No. 5

"(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity.

"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision.

"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest.

"(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage.

"(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official.

"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 18703(c).)

In this case, the economy of the Town of Mammoth Lakes is primarily tourism-based, and the town has a population of approximately 8,000 people. Based upon its relatively small size and tourism-based economy, it appears that at least 25 percent of the businesses in the jurisdiction will be affected by MUF-related decisions. Moreover, to the extent that the effect of the decision is an increase in tourism and not specifically related to the skiing industry, there does not appear to be a unique effect on Councilmember Fernie's business entity, real property, or customers of his rental business. Accordingly, Councilmember Fernie may take part in decisions under the public generally exception.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl
